critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle (EV) programs, including halting development of new EV models and stopping production of the Prologue, a GM-built EV. The author argues this decision risks Honda falling behind in the automotive industry's shift toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles, potentially jeopardizing its future competitiveness.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of its electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and will cease production of the GM-designed Prologue EV.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Honda risks missing out on key industry shifts toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
Legacy automakers like Honda often struggle with EVs because they try to adapt internal combustion engine platforms rather than design EVs from the ground up.
Honda's identity as an internal combustion engine and driver-focused car company is challenged by the rise of EVs and autonomous driving technologies.
Honda's competitiveness is declining, especially in China, where it faces losses and strong competition from newer EV manufacturers.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims align with publicly available information as of early 2026, including reports from Automotive News about Honda halting EV projects and discontinuing the Prologue. The challenges faced by legacy automakers in transitioning to EVs and software-defined vehicles are widely documented in industry analysis. Honda's financial struggles and competitive pressures in China are also supported by recent earnings reports. However, the article presents a critical perspective without citing multiple sources or perspectives, relying heavily on analysis and opinion from the author.
considerations: The article does not provide direct statements or responses from Honda beyond referencing their earnings report.
It lacks perspectives from industry analysts, Honda executives, or other stakeholders that might contextualize Honda's strategic decisions.
The financial and strategic implications for Honda's global operations beyond China are not deeply explored.
Potential plans Honda may have for future EV or SDV development beyond the current halted projects are not discussed.
The article does not consider the broader market or regulatory factors influencing Honda's decisions in detail.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to scale back its electric vehicle (EV) efforts, with many commenters expressing concern that Honda risks falling behind in the automotive industry's shift towards software-defined vehicles and electrification. Some highlight the challenges of EV adoption, such as charging infrastructure and software reliability, while others defend Honda's cautious approach or criticize the current state of EV technology and market readiness.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time.

No thank you. Not sure why the author frames this as a good thing. They've been bamboozled by the automakers and have got it backwards - you're buying a vehicle that already has the capabilities, but are disabled, then paying rent (or a fee) to turn them on. I'm much more likely to buy from a manufacturer that doesn't play these games.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the risk Honda faces by not embracing software-defined vehicles, a key trend in the automotive industry, and critiques the business model of enabling features via paid software.

comments.author: TrackerFF
comments.text:
I live in a top EV market, Norway.

ICE cars have been planned out for years now, and something like 96% of all new cars in Norway were EV last year.

Basically, if you plan on keeping selling ICE cars, you're removing yourself from the market here. There's no future for new personal ICE cars here.

I figure most other countries will be the same.
comments.analysis: Provides a real-world example of a market where EV adoption is dominant, supporting the argument that ICE vehicles have limited future viability in such regions.

comments.author: jedberg
comments.text:
This is so unfortunate. I was never a van guy, but my wife insisted we get a van, so I got the Honda. And honestly? I kinda love it. It drives like a car but holds eight people (or four people and a whole bunch of luggage).

The way we use the van, 90% of our drives are under 20 miles round trip. The rest are longer road trips. I've been waiting eight years for Honda to make an electric or even a plug-in hybrid where the gas motor just charge the battery.

It would be perfect for my family. I guess that's not happening now.
comments.analysis: Offers a personal perspective on the lack of suitable Honda EVs for family use, highlighting a gap in Honda's EV offerings that may affect consumer adoption.

comments.author: pclowes
comments.text:
Everyone is saying EVs are the future but most EVs cannot compete with many of Honda’s offerings.

Eg. I need to move 6 people and significant gear (skiing, camping, biking etc) long remote distances.

There is no EV that can do that really. And the ones that come close are easily $20-30k higher than an Odyssey. Plus the durability of large EVs is far from proven while the 300k mile club of Odyssey owners is large.

I need Suburban/Minivan functionality out of a proven OEM at a competitive price point. (I also need to see my friends with Rivians etc not having to schedule their vacation around charger availability. Have seen this waste hours and hours of time)
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about current EV limitations in terms of capacity, range, and cost compared to traditional vehicles, especially for specific use cases like large families and long trips.

comments.author: bryanlarsen
comments.text:
OTOH, it really looks like Toyota is Goldilocks. Most companies invested too much too early and had to write off a substantial amount, but Toyota is rolling into 2027 with a small but nice selection of EV's.

Over 25% of vehicles sold world-wide were electric in 2025, and that percentage is steadily increasing. So VW & Ford were "too hot", Honda is looking like "too cold" and Toyota might be the "just right" of the three bears.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced industry perspective, comparing automakers' EV strategies and suggesting Toyota's moderate approach may be more sustainable than Honda's retreat or others' aggressive investments.

comments.author: haritha-j
comments.text:
> SDVs don’t have to be EVs, but they tend to go hand in hand. The large battery in an EV makes it easier to feed powerful computers, and it allows things like over-the-air updates to happen when the car is parked and “off.” Could Honda make a fossil fuel SDV? Sure, but it’s unlikely to for the same reason it’s backing away from EVs: The old way of doing things is easier and more profitable, for now.

So can a $300 dollar iPad. Large EV scale batteries are needed to feed powerful computers? What are they on about?
comments.analysis: Clarifies the relationship between EVs and software-defined vehicles, though the comment questioning the battery's role in powering computers suggests some misunderstanding.

comments.author: haritha-j
comments.text: The software defined car practically boils down to this: "It's not quite done yet, but we'll ship it anyway because we can fix it in post". And then two years later, "oh we've already sold them, why spend money updating it to fix the bugs." And five years later, "oh the warranty periods gone, not our problem anymore."
comments.analysis: Critiques the software-defined vehicle rollout strategy, highlighting risks of premature releases and lack of long-term support, which is a common industry concern.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: All the legacy automakers that haven't fully moved to EV's PROFITABLY will go defacto bankrupt within a few years, there will be some mergers to stay alive but it's game over. Tesla and China companies will own auto, with Tesla capturing most the profit, similar to Apple vs Android phones. Autonomy will further accelerate this.
comments.analysis: While somewhat alarmist, this comment reflects a widely held view that legacy automakers must transition to EVs profitably or risk losing market relevance.

comments.author: aetherspawn
comments.text:
Guys, cars are specifically designed to work for their entire life in areas where there is no coverage. Thus, there are plenty of EVs, probably all of them, where you can just open the telematics box and pull the SIM card. Then the software will never update, and the car will just stay in whatever state it’s currently in.

The moment you do this things will stop working: for example phone app, but your car will be more or less unshittified.

And yes, there should probably be a law that makes this easier for the consumer to do for example mandating a plastic hatch or something.

But connected cars are not the end of the world and if we normalise disconnecting cars (make an online list or something of cars that are confirmed to work fine afterwards) then we’ve basically solved the issue. Remember, EVs are not the problem, and this kind of stuff will be mainstream/common knowledge once adoption rates are higher.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical solution to concerns about software updates and connectivity in vehicles, suggesting consumer empowerment could mitigate some risks of software-defined cars.

comments.author: jvillasante
comments.text:
That article reads like a 5 years old wrote it. In particular the part that says "software-defined vehicles is sweeping the automobile industry" and going further by saying "this is what consumers want and expect".

Yeah, if you're 5 and you want to keep paying subscriptions for a car you already bought! Not to mention software failures, over the air updates, hacking, etc

These tech writers (or bros) need to be replaced by AI or something, total disconnection with reality and what a car is for most people (e.g. it's not a computer and it should be mostly about reliability than anything else).
comments.analysis: Expresses strong skepticism about the hype around software-defined vehicles, pointing out real consumer concerns about subscriptions, software failures, and hacking.
